chapters.title: Neurodivergent people are easily affected by their environments
chapters.text:
A person's environment will affect their personality and relationships in many ways. Sensitive, neurodivergent people are no exception, although their case is unique because they experience their environments differently.

The home or work environment can heighten or reduce symptoms of neurodivergence. For instance, research has proven that exposure to air pollution early in life may cause autism. This means that children with the gene can have a higher risk of developing autism when the trait accompanies an increased exposure to air pollution.

It is advised that neurodivergent people study themselves thoroughly and consider what affects their emotional well-being and mental health when designing their home or workspace. For instance, dim lighting with purple or pink pallets is recommended for neurodivergent people.

Smaller, tighter spaces are more desirable and soothing for neurodivergent individuals.

It's best to consult an expert rather than assume based on your physical senses. If you're neurodivergent or know anyone who is, find time to visit experts in that area to seek their career, workplace, and environment recommendations. Some diets and daily habits also favor neurodivergent individuals more than others.

For neurodivergent people to live normally, they need to learn their unique signals and triggers and how they're affected by them. For instance, fast car rides affect many neurodivergent women because of noise and dizziness.

A neurodivergent person notices and gets a quickened heartbeat when experiencing many things that do not disturb a neurotypical person. Helpful procedures neurodivergent people can make use of include:

• Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT)
CBT is a scientific therapy helpful for reframing the situations, behaviors, and interactions of neurodivergent people.

• Medication
This helps neurodivergent people deal with raised sensitivity and its impact.

Did you know? Taking folic acid early in pregnancy may reduce the risk of giving birth to autistic children.

chapters.title: Mental health diagnosis and the study of human behavior have only advanced in the 21st century
chapters.text:
The medical community of the past took issues relating to a woman's mental health lightly. Doctors hardly believed women's complaints, often disregarding them as laziness. People viewed women then as if they were always meant to be healthy.

Since everyone thought of women as stress-free individuals, they paid little attention to them. Many women became prone to medical conditions like depression that often came as a result of emotional abuse. Neurodivergent women numbered many in communities, but doctors could not explain their experiences adequately. Instead, there were a lot of medical assumptions due to ignorance.

One example of this widespread ignorance was seen in the 1930s when people with mental health issues were considered insane. Laws prevented them from getting married to avoid procreating insane children. Another example: the medical community in the 80s believed hysteria was exclusively for single or sexually abused women. But in reality, their hysteria was due to stress in their emotional lives, not restricted to singles or those who suffered abuse.

After years of strenuous research, mental health conditions became medically recognized, but this change didn't happen suddenly. It came as technology advanced, and scientists invented tools capable of examining body parts and physical diseases. Eventually, the fields of psychology and psychiatry started developing, paving the way for more research and discoveries.

As medicine improved in psychiatry and human thoughts, challenges connected with human behavior and the brain were traced to medical conditions, reducing superstition and ignorance.

The world is continuously evolving. The things you think are today’s great technological advancements are nothing compared to what the future holds.

Here's an example to show the progress: in the 80s, ADHD and bipolar disorder became a topic of discussion. The diagnosis of bipolar disorder in children increased in 2000. By 2005, doctors were prescribing antipsychotic drugs to improve children's mental health, and science hasn't looked back.

Did you know? British doctors in the 18th century thought menstruation in women was the cause of mental instability.

chapters.title: Highly sensitive people become depressed whenever they try to blend in
chapters.text:
The first two chapters defined neurodivergence and gave a general and historical overview. The following chapters will cover the most common cases of neurodivergence (HSP, ADHD, autism, SPD). Keep your mind open as we go through these, so you can easily identify and empathize with the different kinds of neurodivergent individuals.

Let's start with highly sensitive people (HSP).

Sensitivity is common in neurodivergent women and indicates a heightened reaction to external conditions like chattering, noise, emotions, and stress.

HSPs usually take a lot of time to perceive and process such external stimuli. They are also easily overwhelmed; other people's mood affects theirs, and they have deeper feelings than usual.

A study led by American Psychologist, Elaine Aron, reveals that about 20% of the population suffers from high sensitivity. HSPs are so different from others that whenever they try to act in a way that seems normal to everyone, they become less productive and depressed. At the same time, they possess a lot of gifts. They are good artists, musicians, psychologists, and entrepreneurs.

HSP is neither a disorder nor a condition. It's simply a personality trait, much like extroversion.

As the knowledge of sensitivity grows, we realize that the concept of normal and abnormal in humans is different and can be wide enough to accommodate neurodivergent people. This can then cause a shift in societal mindset, which can help many undiagnosed neurodivergent individuals get over depression, low self-esteem, and anxiety. So, the language of society must not exclude neurodivergent people.

Having a sensitive nervous system is normal, but what seems ordinary to others, like loud music or crowds, can be highly stimulating and thus stressful for HSPs. ~ Elaine Aron

Jenara Nerenberg

Did you know? Australian sociologist Judy Singer coined the term “neurodiversity” in the late 90s. The concept describes the range of differences in the human brain and regards these differences as normal.

chapters.title: The symptoms of ADHD can be significantly reduced in controlled environments
chapters.text:
ADHD is short for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder. Most adults with it have had it from childhood, and many had close or extended family members with ADHD.

Common signs of ADHD include inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity. Inattention is the inability to focus on one thing at a time, especially in a group or crowded setting with many activities.

Hyperactivity is similar to inattention. It involves the individual being too active for what the social setting “normally” requires. Common examples include fidgeting and talking too much when they should be quiet. The third sign is impulsivity. It is clinically defined as hasty acts that occur without much thought.

These points seem connected, and you can easily identify ADHD in someone with them, right?

Not quite so.

Many people with ADHD can stay calm and focused when immersed in a well-structured environment. This is why it's sometimes hard to notice it in high school students. The high school environment is structured, and students don't have to put much effort into organizing themselves.

Compare that to a college student, where you're basically on your own and, in most cases, separated from your parents. College students have a lot of executive thinking to do, planning their time to cook, study, and engage in social activities. But students with ADHD will easily feel drained in such situations. And it’s the same for adults in professional settings.

People with ADHD tend to exaggerate things without knowing it. One solution the author recommends is for such people to stop being hard on themselves. Instead, they should consider themselves unique and forgive any reaction that doesn't fit into what others expect.

Being hard on yourself because you have a unique personality is a sure way to keep having emotional breakdowns. Learn to excuse your behaviors.

Mental health challenges like ADHD are still ignored in many societies, particularly in the developing world. However, this is the 21st century, and it ought not to be so.

Often what women see as normal in the range of emotional experience has been pathologized. ~ Pauline Meki

Jenara Nerenberg

Did you know? According to WHO's 2017 report, over 6% of the world's population (450 million) have been diagnosed with mental illness.

chapters.title: People suffering from autism and SPD have difficulty learning new things
chapters.text:
Autism is another neurodivergence. This one is quite common, but many people seem uncomfortable with it. Autistic people have been shut out over the years, so society should move toward change by checking our behaviors and reminding ourselves that autism is not inherently bad.

People with autism usually don't do well with speech, audio, visual, and touch stimuli. The autistic experience is quite overwhelming, but fortunately, we're beginning to see the rise of autistic advocates. They work tirelessly to debunk myths regarding autism and help them get accepted in society. For instance, advocates in many parts of the world have helped squash the common belief that autistic people lack empathy.

For people living with autism, sensitivity is a common topic. Sadly, the positive side of being sensitive doesn't get seen often because typical people are busy labeling the traits as “abnormal” or “dysfunctional.”

Some prevalent symptoms of autism include the desire to continually analyze everything, an obsession with certain things, getting lost in thoughts, and imitating people.

Some people believe autistic individuals lack empathy. But this is not true because autism comes with a lot of sensitivity to people and the environment.

SPD

People with SPD (sensory processing disorder) have challenges with processing stimuli and information and difficulty learning new motor tasks. They don't do well in meetings, have difficulty interpreting words and forming thoughts or ideas, and generally demonstrate heightened anxiety.

Women who suffer from SPD lack balance, coordination, and the perfect use of their sense organs. Some forms of SPDs are sensory modulation disorder (SMD) and sensory discrimination disorder (SDD). SMD is characterized by sensory over-responsivity, where individuals are more sensitive to stimuli than others, or sensory under-responsivity, hardly sensitive to stimuli. There are also sensory cravings, a special kind of SMD where people constantly crave sensory stimulation. SDD, on the other hand, is the inability to place stimuli properly. For instance, people with SDD could struggle to understand if they're falling to the side or backward.

Did you know? People with SPD generally tend to be resistant to sex.

chapters.title: Neurodivergent people are easily affected by their environments
chapters.text:
A person's environment will affect their personality and relationships in many ways. Sensitive, neurodivergent people are no exception, although their case is unique because they experience their environments differently.

This means that children with the gene can have a higher risk of developing autism when the trait accompanies an increased exposure to air pollution.

It is advised that neurodivergent people study themselves thoroughly and consider what affects their emotional well-being and mental health when designing their home or workspace. For instance, dim lighting with purple or pink pallets is recommended for neurodivergent people.

Smaller, tighter spaces are more desirable and soothing for neurodivergent individuals.

If you're neurodivergent or know anyone who is, find time to visit experts in that area to seek their career, workplace, and environment recommendations. This helps neurodivergent people deal with raised sensitivity and its impact.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Neurodivergence is a term associated with people whose brains are differently built. Unfortunately, ignorance and medical negligence have made the rest of the world believe people with neurodivergent brains are misfits. But science has proven this false; they are not abnormal, just different. As a matter of fact, millions of neurodivergent people have incredible gifts and excel because of their unique brains.

One thing that makes neurodivergent people unique is that they tend to have higher levels of empathy. For instance, neurodivergent employees are the best to reach out to when trying to discover the thoughts of other employees toward the company.

Artificial intelligence (AI) can help further develop this giftedness and empathy in neurodivergent people, but it’s quite expensive, and few families can afford it.

However, our collective effort as a people to make society a liveable place for everyone is affordable. Adjusting the tone of the community and creating a conducive environment for neurodivergent people will help them avoid getting territorial or defensive and instead focus on just being themselves. This positive shift has started in many parts of the world, thanks to neurodiversity movements and authors like Jenara Nerenberg. The Internet has also helped further spread the word.

Be determined to become one of those agents of change that society needs. You can start by wholeheartedly accepting the neurodivergent people around you and encouraging them to embrace their uniqueness.

Try this

ADHD is characterized by inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity. See if you can observe these traits in yourself or someone around you.

You could also see the following movies to help you learn more about neurodivergence: I Am Sam, Rain Man, A Beautiful Mind, What's Eating Gilbert Grape?
